mnemonics.title: STAGRR - The Six Constraints
mnemonics.content: S - Scope, T - Time, A - Assets (Resources), G - Goals (Quality), R - Risk, R - Revenue (Cost). Remember: STAGRR helps remember the 6 key constraints in software project management.

mnemonics.title: PRINCE2 Key Themes
mnemonics.content: P - Progress, R - Risk, I - Issue, C - Change, E - Quality, 2 - Organization, Plans. A project management methodology reminder.

mnemonics.title: Project Manager's ABCs
mnemonics.content: A - Assess, B - Balance, C - Communicate. Assess risks, Balance constraints, Communicate with stakeholders - the three pillars of effective SPM.

acronyms.usage: The discipline of managing software development projects
Guide by PMI defining project management standards
Decomposing project scope into manageable work packages
Document defining all requirements for the software project
Document soliciting bids from vendors for project work
Measure of financial benefit relative to project cost
Technique measuring project performance and progress

visualTips.visualization: Draw a triangle with 'Scope' at top, 'Time' on bottom-left, and 'Cost' on bottom-right. Show arrows connecting them to represent how changing one affects others. Quality sits in the center as the outcome.
Use a wave or S-curve showing: Initiation (low effort, high uncertainty) → Planning (increasing effort) → Execution (peak effort) → Monitoring (steady) → Closure (declining effort).
Create a 2x2 matrix: High Power/High Interest (Manage Closely), High Power/Low Interest (Keep Satisfied), Low Power/High Interest (Keep Informed), Low Power/Low Interest (Monitor).
Balance scale: On one side 'Risk', on other 'Return'. As risk increases, potential return increases. Projects must find optimal balance based on organizational risk tolerance.

analogies.analogy: Building a house (construction) is like following a blueprint - you can see progress, materials are tangible. Writing software is like writing a novel - the product exists only in the author's mind until completed, and plot (requirements) can change mid-story.

Just as a conductor coordinates different instruments to create symphony, a project manager coordinates team members with different skills to deliver a successful project - all playing the same 'score' (plan) but producing harmonious results.

Like planning a simple birthday party but gradually adding a magician, photo booth, and live band without increasing budget or time - the scope expands beyond what was originally planned, eventually causing chaos.

Think of ordering food: if you want fast (Time) and cheap (Cost), you accept limited menu (Scope). Want fast and variety? Pay more. Want cheap and variety? Wait longer. You can't have all three perfectly - software projects work the same way.
